claim: Dogs presenting to UK veterinary practices with heat-related illness show altered respiration/panting in 68.73% of cases and lethargy in 47.79%, making these the dominant early clinical signs.
stance_target: field-observable behavioral signs for identifying heat-stressed lost dogs before collapse
scope_conditions: 856 HRI events in primary-care UK veterinary practices, 2016–2018
quoted_support: the most commonly recorded clinical signs were respiratory changes (68.73%) and lethargy (47.79%).

claim: Severe HRI has a case fatality rate of 56.76% in dogs, compared to 5.46% for moderate and 2.21% for mild cases.
stance_target: time-to-intervention is critical: severity escalates rapidly and late-stage HRI is frequently fatal
scope_conditions: 856 HRI events graded using VetCompass tool, UK primary care 2016–2018
quoted_support: The fatality rate (death overall) for canine HRI events classified using the novel VetCompass HRI Grading Tool was 2.21% for mild events, 5.46% for moderate events and 56.76% for severe events.

claim: Dogs presenting with body temperature ≥43°C have a relative risk of death 7.98x higher than those with lower temperatures; ≥41°C raises risk 4.23x.
stance_target: the 41–43°C range is the actionable danger threshold for triage decisions
scope_conditions: 629 HRI events with body temperature recorded at presentation, UK primary care
quoted_support: Dogs presenting with a body temperature ≥ 43 °C had a higher relative risk of death and unassisted death compared to dogs with a temperature ≥ 41 °C.

claim: Neurological signs (unresponsive, coma, stupor, multiple seizures) are the strongest predictors of death in canine HRI, with unresponsive dogs showing 84.31% fatality.
stance_target: neurological status is the most important triage indicator in field HRI assessment
scope_conditions: 793 HRI events with recorded clinical signs
quoted_support: Dogs presenting with abnormal mentation (including unresponsive, coma, stupor, multiple seizures and status epilepticus), gastrointestinal haemorrhage and petechiae/purpura had at least three times the relative risk of death compared to dogs presenting without those clinical signs.

claim: Almost one-third (30%) of canine HRI cases had no specific trigger documented, suggesting owners often fail to recognize heat exposure as the precipitating cause.
stance_target: rescue responders should consider HRI even when the owner/finder cannot identify a clear heat event
scope_conditions: UK primary care dogs with HRI, 2016–2018
quoted_support: No specific triggers for HRI were reported in the clinical records for almost a third of the dogs presented in the primary-care study, potentially demonstrating a lack of recognition of early signs of HRI by owners or veterinary professionals.
